segments.s311-g17: رسیدم is 1st singular past of رسیدن = "I arrived/ reached." بدین دژ is the prepositional phrase 'به + این + دژ' = 'to/at this fortress' (the input splits it as ب + دین + دژ; treat them together as the demonstrative phrase). که introduces a relative/nominal clause 'which/where', and افراسیاب is the subject of that clause (proper name).
segments.s311-g18: همی داشت is a poetic imperfective/continuous past construction: همی (continuous particle) + داشت (past of داشتن used as imperfective auxiliary) = 'was keeping / had been keeping.' از بهر is a purpose phrase meaning 'for the sake of / for'; آرام و خواب are the purposes: 'rest/peace and sleep.'
segments.s311-g19: بدو اندرون بود is a locative phrase: بدو = 'to/at it' (i.e. 'therein'), اندرون = 'inside/within', and بود is past 'there was/were.' تخت و کلاه lists two items ('a throne and a helmet'). The word order places the locative phrase before the copula/verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s311-g20: This segment is a simple enumerative list of possessions/qualities: بزرگی = 'greatness/grandeur', دیهیم is an archaic/poetic term meaning 'the rural people / villagers' or 'folk', and گنج و سپاه = 'treasure and an army.' The conjunction و simply links the items.
segments.s311-g21: چهل پیل = 'forty elephants' (numeral before noun). ز ایشان = 'of them' (poetic/genitive preposition). همه emphasizes 'all/every one.' بسته گشت is a compound meaning 'became bound' — گشت (become) yields the resultive sense 'were bound' here.
segments.s311-g22: هر آن کس که برگشت = 'whoever/each person who returned' (هر آن کس که is a common indefinite/relative pattern). تن خسته گشت = literally 'the body became tired,' an idiomatic way to say the person grew weary; گشت again = 'became.'
segments.s311-g23: بگوید here is a narrative/epic verbal form meaning 'he says / he tells' (3rd person); the provided lexical senses list a noun sense, so no exact match is given in the senses list. کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb). گیو is the speaker's name. یک‌یک = 'one by one' (adverbial, modifying the telling). به شاه marks the indirect object 'to the king.' Word order: temporal adverb + subject + verb, with adverbial modifiers placed nearby.
segments.s311-g24: سخن = 'speech/account/tale' (direct object). هرچه رفت = 'whatever went/happened' — here رفت functions as 'took place / happened' (past event), with هرچه introducing 'whatever/all that.' اندر این رزمگاه is the locative 'in this battlefield,' modifying the preceding clause.
segments.s311-g129: به یک هفته از کاخ کاووس کی is a temporal-location phrase plus a conjunction. به یک هفته از کاخ کاووس = “one week (after/of) (at) Kavus’s palace,” where به + (time span) marks the time frame; از introduces the source/place (از کاخ کاووس = “from/at the palace of Kavus”). کی is the old/poetic temporal conjunction meaning “when.” Word order is topical: the time/place is put first, then the temporal conjunction.
segments.s311-g130: همی موج برخاست از جام می is the main clause: همی is a classical adverbial particle (here marking ongoing/regular or simple narration, often translated as “then/thereupon/again”); موج (subject) + برخاست (past tense verb ‘rose/was raised’) form the predicate; از جام می is a prepositional complement ‘from the cup of wine’ with جام (cup) governing می (wine). The verb برخاست is in simple past narrative tense.
segments.s311-g131: سر ماه نو خلعت گیو ساخت is a temporal phrase followed by object and verb. سر ماه نو = “at the head/beginning of the new month” (سر used temporally as ‘start’ + ماه نو ‘new month’). خلعت گیو is the direct object phrase ‘a robe (of honour) for Giv’ (object noun before the verb). ساخت is the past tense verb ‘made/gave’. Classical Persian often places the object before the verb.
segments.s311-g132: همی زر و پیروزه اندر نشاخت modifies the previous clause: همی again a narrative adverb, زر و پیروزه (‘gold and turquoise’) are the materials listed, اندر is the locative/prepositional particle meaning ‘in/into/on’, and نشاخت is an archaic/poetic verb here meaning ‘set/ornamented/embroidered’ (the listed senses indicate an archaic form). The phrase therefore says he adorned/inserted gold and turquoise into it.
segments.s311-g133: طبق‌های زرین و پیروزه جام lists objects with postpositive adjectives. طبق‌های زرین = ‘golden plates/trays’ (note the plural suffix -های), و پیروزه جام = ‘and the turquoise cup’ (here the adjective/qualifier follows the noun, a common classical order). The conjunction و links two noun phrases.
segments.s311-g134: کمرهای زرین و زرین ستام continues the list: کمرهای زرین = ‘golden girdles/belts’ (noun + adjective), then و connects to زرین ستام, where زرین again qualifies ستام. The exact nuance of ستام here is noun/participial and the phrase is a terse, parallel list (adjectives and nouns are often repeated for poetic rhythm).
segments.s311-g135: پرستار با طوق و با گوشوار is an instrumental/comitative phrase describing who is present: پرستار (attendants/attendant‑servants) followed by two repeated با phrases listing items they wear: با طوق ‘with a necklace’ و با گوشوار ‘and with an earring’. Repeating با before each item is a classical stylistic choice emphasizing each adornment.
segments.s311-g136: همان یاره و تاج گوهر نگار: همان ‘the same’ points back to previously mentioned persons, یاره names them as companions/attendants, و links to تاج گوهر نگار, where تاج is the noun ‘crown’ and گوهر نگار is a compound modifier meaning ‘adorned/inscribed with gems’ (noun then modifier produces a jewel‑studded sense). The whole segment appends the crowned ornament to the listed attendants.
segments.s311-g57: فرود آمد is a compound past tense meaning 'he descended / alighted'; the subject is omitted (third person). از + noun phrase marks source ('from the throne of Kavus, the king'), where تخت کاووس شاه is a genitive chain 'the throne of Kavus, the king'. Word order is typical Persian (preposition + NP), and the whole segment is a single past action.
segments.s311-g58: ز سر برگرفت is literal 'he took (it) from the head'; برگرفت is past simple 'took up / removed'. ز سر is a prepositional phrase 'off/from the head'. آن کیانی کلاه is the demonstrative + adjectival phrase 'that Kayanian cap' (کیانی qualifying کلاه). The verb refers back to the same (omitted) subject who descended in the previous segment.
segments.s311-g59: بیامد is a past narrative 'he came'. بغلتید is a past verb meaning 'he rolled/ threw himself (upon)' — here an intransitive reflexive action used with بر + NP to indicate falling or casting oneself onto something. بر تیره خاک is the prepositional phrase 'upon the dark earth', with تیره as an attributive adjective.
segments.s311-g60: نیایش کنان is an adverbial participle ('praying' or 'engaged in prayer') formed from the noun + کنان (a poetic participial form) and modifies the main verb (shows manner). پیش یزدان پاک is a prepositional phrase 'before the Pure God' (پیش = in the presence of / before), with پاک qualifying یزدان.
segments.s311-g61: وز here functions as the conjunctive contraction 'و ز' (and from/and then) introducing the result. آن جایگه شد is 'from that place he became' (شد as past 'became' or auxiliary), and به جای نشست is a fixed expression 'sat in his place / took his seat' (نشست past 'sat'). The pair شد ... نشست conveys he came to be seated.
segments.s311-g62: به گرد دژ is a locative phrase 'around the fortress' (به گرد = around/about). آیین شادی ببست uses ببست (past of بستن) in a poetic/administrative sense here meaning 'he set up / established / held' the ritual/ceremony of joy (آیین شادی). The verb is past and the whole clause describes arranging festivities around the citadel.
segments.s311-g63: همی is the archaic/emphatic particle that precedes a verb to indicate continuous or habitual action ('kept on / was continually'). گفت here functions as the verb 'said / spoke' (the provided lemma senses are nominal but the sense used is the verbal one), so همی گفت با شاه گیو = 'he kept speaking with the king Giv'. آنچه دید is a free relative 'what he saw' (آنچه + past verb دید).
segments.s311-g64: سخن کز لب شاه ایران شنید is a noun (سخن) followed by a relative clause introduced by کز ('which/from which') — 'the words which he heard from the lip(s) of the King of Iran'. لب شاه ایران is a genitive chain 'the lip of the King of Iran' and شنید is past 'he heard'. The whole phrase specifies the content Giv reports.
segments.s311-g137: همان is a demonstrative meaning “that/the same.” جامه is the noun “robe/garment,” headed by همان. تخت here names the function/recipient (“for the throne”) rather than a bed — it qualifies جامه. افگندنی is an infinitive/adjectival form (archaic/poetic -نی) meaning “to be cast/spread”; together تخت و افگندنی describe what kind of robe it is (fit for the throne and to be spread).
segments.s311-g138: ز is the poetic preposition “of/from” and is repeated for rhythmic listing: ز رنگ و ز بو = “of color and of fragrance.” وز in the manuscript is the fused form (و + ز) used in poetry to continue the list (“and of …”); پراگندنی is an adjectival/participle form meaning “scattered/strewn,” further describing the robe. The whole segment lists qualities of the garment.
segments.s311-g139: فرستاد is a simple past “sent.” تا introduces the purpose/result clause here = “so that / in order that.” گیو را is the definite object (را marks Giv as the one affected). خواندند is past plural “they called/summoned” (here sense = to summon), completing the purpose clause.
segments.s311-g140: بر is the preposition “upon/on.” اورنگ is “throne.” زرینش is ‘golden’ with the suffix -ش marking possession/association (his/their golden). بنشاندند is past plural “they seated/installed” — the subject (attendants) is omitted but understood; the object (Giv) has been marked earlier (گیو را).
segments.s311-g141: ببردند is past plural “they carried/they took.” خلعت is the robe of honour (object). به نزدیک + اوی is a locative phrase “to/near him”; اوی is an older/poetic third‑person pronoun meaning “him/his.” Word order (verb before object and locative) is typical of the epic style.
segments.s311-g142: بمالید is past “they rubbed/smeared/anointed” (here transitive: they anointed someone). The following word گیو is the patient/recipient (object), placed after the verb by poetic inversion. اندر means “in/on/within”; آن تخت روی is “that throne’s surface” or loosely “upon that throne” (روی = surface/face). Together: they anointed Giv on the throne.
segments.s311-g41: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. 'گیو' is the proper-name subject. 'اندر آمد' is an archaic compound meaning 'entered/came in' (اندر + آمد), with آمد in the simple past. 'به نزدیک شاه' is a prepositional phrase ('to/near the king') where به + نزدیک = 'toward/near' and شاه is the object of the phrase.
segments.s311-g42: زمین را is the direct object marked by the enclitic object/definiteness marker را. ببوسید is a past-tense verb 'he kissed'. بر پیش گاه is a locative phrase: بر + پیش (here 'in the presence of') + گاه (literally 'occasion/seat' but forming the fixed notion 'presence/court'); together it means 'before/in the presence of (the king)'.
segments.s311-g43: ورا is a fronted third-person object pronoun 'to/at him', placed before the verb for emphasis. دید is the past tense 'saw'. کاووس is the subject (Kavus); Persian here permits object–verb–subject order for emphasis. بر پای جست is a verb phrase 'sprang to (his) feet' where بر marks direction/toward and جست is the past of 'to leap'.
segments.s311-g44: بخندید is simple past 'he laughed'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بسترد (from ستر/سترد) is an older/petoetic past form meaning 'he covered/concealed'. رویش is روی ('face') plus the third-person possessive clitic ش 'his'. به دست is an instrumental/with-phrase ('with his hand') where به marks the instrument.
segments.s311-g45: بپرسیدش is a past-tense verb 'he asked him' (بپرسید + -ش object clitic). از here functions as 'about/concerning' (asking about someone). شهریار و سپاه is the coordinated object: 'the king and the army'.
segments.s311-g46: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing a descriptive/genitive phrase. گردنده and تابنده are participial/adjectival forms: گردنده 'circling/turning' and تابنده 'shining/resplendent', each modifying خورشید (sun) and ماه (moon) respectively; the phrase is a poetic epithet describing the army/king in terms of sun and moon imagery.
segments.s311-g47: بگفت is past 'he said'. آن is a demonstrative 'that' or 'the (aforementioned)'. کجا is the interrogative 'where'. دید is past 'saw/beheld'. گیو is the proper name; سترگ is an epithet attached to Giv meaning 'of broad sight' or 'far-seeing' (an attributive noun/adjective derived from 'sight'). Note the compact, somewhat inverted order typical of epic diction.
segments.s311-g48: ز is again the poetic 'from/of'. گردان here is the noun 'troop/host' (the army). وز in the manuscript appears to be the fused sequence 'و ز' (= 'and from'), but the listed senses for وز do not match this use (so sense selection is not available). شهریار بزرگ = 'the great king' (شهریار 'king' modified by بزرگ 'great').
segments.s311-g9: Nominal clause with an emphatic pronoun subject: همو (emphatic 'he himself') precedes the predicate noun آفریننده ('creator'). Persian omits a copula here, so the sense is 'He is the creator of X and Y.' The coordinate phrase پیل و مور lists two direct objects of آفریننده.
segments.s311-g10: A prepositional range introduced by ز ... تا: ز خاشاک تا ... = 'from chaff up to/ until ...'. آب دریای شور is a genitive noun phrase ('water of the salt sea') where دریای modifies آب; شور qualifies دریا as 'salt/salty.'
segments.s311-g11: همه is the subject ('all/everyone'); با توانایی او is an instrumental/agent phrase ('by/with his power') modifying how they are one; یکیست is a nominal predicate 'is one' (یکی + copula) stating unity. Word order is Subject + adjunct + predicate.
segments.s311-g12: Two coordinate clauses repeating the subject for emphasis: 'خداوند هست' ('the Lord exists') and 'خداوند نیست' ('the Lord does not exist'). هست and نیست are present-tense copulas/verbs; the contrast is rhetorical/paradoxical.
segments.s311-g13: Relative clause: کسی را که ... = 'the person whom ...'; را marks کس as definite/definite object of the main clause. او پروراند is past tense ('he reared/raised'); به مهر ('with affection') is an instrumental phrase modifying how he raised them.
segments.s311-g14: Main clause: بر آن کس = 'upon that person'; نگردد is the negative present/subjunctive form of گشت/گرد (here impersonal sense 'befall'/'turn upon') — so 'does not befall/shall not turn upon.' به تندی سپهر is an adverbial phrase 'with the swiftness/ferocity of the sky' (i.e. the heavens' fury). Word order places the oblique phrase before the verb.
segments.s311-g15: از او ('from him') gives the source; باد here functions as an optative particle meaning 'may there be' (a common classical Persian prayer formula). بر شاه گیتی درود = 'blessings/greetings upon the king of the world.' The literal order is Source + optative + goal + noun.
segments.s311-g16: کز او = 'from him' (کز = از که, 'from which/whom'); خیزد is a present/esthetic verb 'arises/comes forth'; آرام را تار و پود is an object phrase where آرام ('peace/calm') is marked by را (definite object) and تار و پود ('warp and weft') define its fabric — 'the warp and weft of peace.'
segments.s311-g81: همان is a demonstrative pointing back to a previously mentioned person (“that/the same”); it precedes the names. جهن and گرسیوز are proper names, joined by the coordinating و ‘and’. بندسای is an epithet formed as a noun meaning ‘binder’/‘fetter‑maker’ that follows the name it qualifies.
segments.s311-g82: که introduces a relative clause (‘who/that’). او is the third‑person pronoun ‘he’. برد here is the past of بردن ‘to take/drag’ (not the lexical senses listed), followed by پای ‘foot’ as the thing by which Siyâvash was taken; سیاوش is the name, ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’, and جای is ‘place’. The clause gives the action done by those named.
segments.s311-g83: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. گرسیوز is the subject. بدکنش is a compound noun ‘ill‑doer / the wrongdoer’ and را is the definite object marker attaching to it. بدید is the past tense ‘saw’ (so: ‘when Garsivaz saw the evildoer’).
segments.s311-g84: بر او کرد نفرین is a verb phrase: بر او ‘upon/against him’ + کرد (did/made) + نفرین (a curse) – ‘he laid a curse upon him’. The following که introduces the content clause (‘that…’), and سزید is a past form from سزا ‘to befit/deserve’ meaning ‘was deserved’ or ‘was fitting’, so the clause states the curse was deserved.
segments.s311-g85: همان again points back (‘that’). جهن is the personal name and را marks the whole person as definite object. پای کرده به بند is a causative/compound expression: پای کردن به بند = ‘to make (someone) foot‑bound’, i.e. ‘to fetter by the feet’; کرده is a past participial/finite form ‘having made/bound’. به introduces the instrument/place (‘into/into the fetter’).
segments.s311-g86: ببردند is a past plural verb ‘they took/brought’ (archaic/formal past). It is followed by نزدیک ‘near’ and the noun phrase تخت بلند ‘the high/throne (literally ‘throne high’)’ as the destination/adverbial phrase: ‘they led him near the high throne’. Word order places the verb before the final locative phrase.
segments.s311-g87: بدان here functions as a prepositional/compound particle meaning ‘to/at those’ or ‘by those’ (classical usage). دختران is the plural noun ‘maidens/daughters’. رد functions as ‘passing/byway’ (i.e. ‘passed by/among’). افراسیاب is the subject performing the action: ‘Afrasiab passed by those maidens.’
segments.s311-g88: نگه کرد is a verbal phrase ‘(he) looked/gazed’ (کرد used as the light verb with نگه). کاووس is the subject here. مژگان ‘eyelashes’ is the possessor phrase with پر آب (‘full of water/tears’): پر is the adjective ‘full (of)’, آب literally ‘water’ used figuratively as ‘tears’ — ‘Kavus looked, his eyelashes full of tears.’
segments.s311-g49: The clause begins with the predicate 'جوان شد' (became young) followed by the cause phrase 'ز گفتار او' (because of/from his speech) and then the subject 'مرد پیر' (the old man). This inverted order (predicate + adverbial + subject) is common in poetry to emphasize the change; 'شد' is the past-tense/resultative verb 'became'.
segments.s311-g50: 'پس' is a discourse adverb meaning 'then', followed by the demonstrative 'آن' ('that') modifying 'نامه' (the letter). 'بنهاد' is a classical past tense (perfective) of نهادن 'to put/place'; here it governs the following prepositional phrase 'پیش دبیر' ('before/in the presence of the scribe'), where 'پیش' means 'in front of; before' and 'دبیر' names the court scribe/secretary as the indirect object. The clause is a simple past action.
segments.s311-g51: 'چو' introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. The noun phrase 'آن نامه‌بر' ('that letter‑carrier/messenger') is the subject, 'شاه ایران' is the indirect object/target of the reading (here 'to the King of Iran'), and 'بخواند' is the past-tense verb 'read/recited' (3rd person singular perfective in epic style). The clause describes the triggering action.
segments.s311-g52: This main clause states the reaction: 'همه انجمن' ('the whole assembly/everyone') is the subject, 'در شگفتی' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in astonishment/wonder', and 'بماند' is the past/resultative verb 'remained' or 'was left'. The syntax is straightforward S V with a locative/state phrase indicating their condition.
segments.s311-g53: 'همه' (everyone) is the subject for two coordinated resultative predicates: 'شاد گشتند' ('became glad') and 'خرم شدند' ('became joyful'). Both verbs are past plural forms ('گشتند', 'شدند') describing the change of state; the coordination with 'و' links the two related emotional states.
segments.s311-g54: The causal prepositional phrase 'ز شادی' ('from/because of joy') introduces the result clause. 'دو دیده' ('two eyes' = both eyes) is the grammatical subject, 'پر از نم' is a nominal phrase 'full of moisture/tears' and 'شدند' is the past plural copular/resultative 'became' (they became full). Overall: 'both eyes became full of tears because of joy.'
segments.s311-g55: The clause 'همه چیز دادند درویش را' is object-final with the verb 'دادند' (they gave) and the definite object marker 'را' after 'درویش', so syntactically it reads 'They gave everything to the dervish.' 'همه چیز' is the direct object (everything) and 'درویش را' is the beneficiary marked with 'را' (typical epic stylistic order).
segments.s311-g56: 'بنفریده' is a proper name here (a person presented/appointed), 'کردند' is the past plural 'they made/did', and 'بدکیش را' with the object marker 'را' names the person affected: 'the ill‑fated one'. The clause thus means roughly 'they made (so-and-so) the ill‑fated one / they called him Bad‑kish,' with the verb 'کردند' performing the change of status.
segments.s311-g105: به + proper name + آمد is a simple motion clause: به (preposition 'to') marks the destination, گرسیوز is the place-name, and آمد is the past-tense verb 'came'. چنان جای بهر is a short nominal phrase appended in poetic order: چنان 'such' qualifies جای 'place', and بهر ('for/for the sake of') indicates purpose or characterization — together meaning 'for such a place' or 'to such a place' (poetic word order places this descriptive phrase after the verb).
segments.s311-g106: چنینست is a fixed copular/predicative phrase 'it is thus / such is'. کردار is the noun 'deed / conduct' and گردنده is an attributive present participle 'turning, revolving', modifying دهر 'the age/world'. Together کردار گردنده دهر = 'the conduct of the revolving world'; the whole clause states a general truth: 'such is the conduct of the turning world.'
segments.s311-g107: خنک here functions as a predicate/adjective meaning 'fortunate/blessed'. آن کسی کو is a relative phrase 'that person who' (poetic کو = 'who'), followed by بود (past copula 'was') and پادشا 'king' as the predicate. Word order is emphatic/poetic: adjective + relative phrase + copula + predicate.
segments.s311-g108: کفی راد consists of کفی 'palm/hand' qualified by راد 'generous/bountiful' (a kenning for generosity). دارد is the present tense 'has/possesses', and دلی پارسا is a noun phrase 'a pious heart' functioning as another thing possessed. The sequence means 'he has a generous hand and a pious heart.'
segments.s311-g109: بداند is third-person present (classical narrative form) 'he knows'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...', and گیتی بر او بگذرد is a verb phrase with بگذرد (present/subjunctive of 'to pass') meaning 'the world passes over/against him' — i.e. 'he knows that worldly affairs will pass him by.'
segments.s311-g110: نگردد is negative + present/subjunctive of گردیدن 'to turn/change' meaning 'does not turn' or 'does not change'. In the poetic word order the subject (بی‌خرد 'the foolish/unwise one') appears at the end; به گرد در is a prepositional cluster literally 'to/around the circle/door' but idiomatically marks a turning or change of direction — here: 'the fool does not change (his ways).'
segments.s311-g111: خرد چون شود is a temporal clause: خرد 'wisdom' + چون 'when/as' + شود 'becomes/arrives' = 'when wisdom comes'. از دو دیده سرشک places the resulting image: از 'from' دو دیده 'the two eyes' + سرشک 'a tear' (in Persian the verb 'to fall' is omitted poetically), so 'a tear from both eyes (is shed)'.
segments.s311-g112: چنان هم که is a comparative 'just so/as', introducing the simile. دیوانه خواهد پزشک is literal: دیوانه 'a madman' as subject, خواهد (present of خواستن) 'wants/desires', and پزشک 'a physician' as the object — 'just as a madman desires a physician.' The whole line compares tears of wisdom to the madman's cry for a healer.
segments.s311-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. باد هوا together refers to the weather/wind (literal 'wind and air'); گشت is the past-tense/resultative 'became/turned'. و simply links the two clauses. ببرید is a poetic/past-form of بردن here meaning 'cleared/took away' (the verb precedes its object, راه), so the phrase reads 'when the wind/air turned and (the obstacles of) the road were carried away / the road was cleared.'
segments.s311-g34: بیامد is simple past 'came'. به here marks direction 'toward/into', نزدیک is the adjective 'near', and کاووس شاه is the king's name plus title (noun phrase in apposition). Word order places the motion verb before the directional phrase, typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s311-g35: پس is a temporal/discourse adverb 'then'. آگاهی here means 'news/information' and آمد is the simple past 'came' used impersonally ('news came/reached'). به marks the indirect object 'to Kavus'. کی is the relative/archaismsense 'who/that' introducing the following description.
segments.s311-g36: از governs the topic/source 'about' or 'concerning' (news about). آن is the demonstrative 'that'. پهلوان زاده is a compound: پهلوان 'champion/hero' + زاده 'born/offspring' (here 'hero-born' or 'son of a hero'). نیک‌پی is an epithet 'well-born/noble' modifying the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s311-g37: پذیره is a noun 'reception/welcome' (here 'welcoming party' or 'reception party'), and فرستاد is the past tense 'sent/dispatched' with پذیره as its object. چندی qualifies سپاه as 'several/a number of' and سپاه means 'army/host' (a contingent sent to receive him).
segments.s311-g38: گرانمایگان is a plural/collective 'the honored/illustrious ones' (distinguished men). بر here means 'toward/to' (marking motion onto a route). گرفتند is the past 'they took' and combines with راه 'road' (راه is the object), so گرفتند راه means '(they) took the road' = 'set out'.
segments.s311-g39: چو is 'when' introducing a temporal clause. آمد is past 'came'. بر before شهر marks direction 'to/into' (arrived at). گیو is the proper name and دلیر is an epithet 'brave' following the name (adjective after noun, common in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s311-g40: سپاهی is a collective noun 'a host/army'. ز is the poetic form of از 'from', linking گردان 'troops/companies' as the source ('a host from the battalions'). چو is the simile 'like/as', and یک دشت شیر is a vivid metaphor 'a whole plain of lions' (دشت 'plain' + شیر 'lions') comparing the host's might.
segments.s311-g113: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'from then on' (از = from; آن پس = after that). کز introduces a subordinate clause 'that/when' and here has the sense 'that/when'; the verb بپرداخت (past) follows the indirect object ایشان ('them') in an archaic word order, with شاه placed after the verb as the logical subject. The clause therefore reads literally 'from that after that that from them turned away the king' = 'from that time when the king turned away from them.'
segments.s311-g114: ز بیگانه مردم تهی کرد is literal 'he made the people empty of strangers': ز = 'of/from' marking what the people are being emptied of, بیگانه = 'foreigners/strangers', مردم = 'the people', تهی = 'devoid (of)', and کرد is the past tense causative 'made/caused' (i.e. 'deprived'). گاه at the end is an adverb 'occasionally/at times' modifying the action.
segments.s311-g115: نویسنده is the agent 'the scribe/copyist'. آهنگ here means 'intention/aim' (not musical tune) and is used with قرطاس ('paper, the sheet') as the object: literally 'the scribe made/put (his) intention on the paper' — an idiom meaning he prepared to write. کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/made'.
segments.s311-g116: سر خامه = 'the head/tip of the quill (pen)'; بر سان = an archaic simile-marker 'on/like' (بر = on/upon; سان = 'like/as'), and الماس کرد = 'made (it) a diamond' or 'made it like a diamond' (کرد = 'made'). The clause thus reads 'he made the pen's tip like a diamond' (a poetic image of a sharp, bright nib).
segments.s311-g117: نبشتند is past plural 'they wrote'. نامه is the direct object 'letters'. The recipient phrase به هر کشوری follows: به = 'to', هر = 'every', کشوری = 'country/realm' (here 'to every country'). The verb-subject order (verb before explicit subject elsewhere) is typical of the epic/poetic style.
segments.s311-g118: به هر نامداری و هر مهتری continues the list of recipients: به = 'to', هر = 'every', نامداری here refers to 'the illustrious/notable (i.e. famous persons)', و = 'and', مهتری = 'chiefs/elders'. The whole phrase means 'to every notable and every chief.'
segments.s311-g119: که شد introduces a result clause ('so that' / 'and it happened that'). ترک و چین = 'the Turks and China' (i.e. the peoples of Turkestan and China) as the subject; شاه را uses the object/experiencer marker را with 'king' to indicate the one affected, and یکسره 'completely' qualifies the change. The idiomatic sense is that Turks and Chinese deserted/ceased to be under the king entirely (شد = 'became/happened' used to mark this change of state).
segments.s311-g120: به آبشخور آمد is the verb phrase 'came to the watering-place' (به = 'to', آبشخور = 'watering-place', آمد = 'came'); پلنگ و بره are the subjects 'the leopard and the lamb' (joined by و). The inversion (verb before subject) is a poetic ordering; the image indicates peace or a marvelous harmony — predator and prey coming to drink together.
segments.s311-g1: بفرمود تا introduces a commanded subordinate clause: بفرمود (honorific past) = 'he ordered/commanded'. تا here means 'so that/that' and marks the content of the order. پیش او شد دبیر is an impersonal/causative-like phrasing meaning 'a scribe was made to appear before him' (شد used as auxiliary/passive-like 'be/come to be' and دبیر is the agent 'scribe'). Word order places the subordinate clause after تا, with پیش او (in his presence) before شد دبیر.
segments.s311-g2: بیاورد is a third-person subjunctive/optative used in the subordinate clause after تا, meaning 'that he should bring' or 'to bring'. The direct objects follow: قرطاس (paper) و چینی حریر is a noun phrase 'and Chinese silk' (چینی modifies حریر = 'Chinese silk'). The verb governs both objects.
segments.s311-g3: یکی نامه = 'a certain letter' (یکی = 'a certain / one'). از introduces the material/ointment sense here: 'نامه از قیر و مشک و گلاب' = 'a letter [with/of] tar, musk and rosewater' (classical phrasing that describes how the letter was prepared or scented). The list of substances is joined by و.
segments.s311-g4: بفرمود again functions as 'he ordered/caused' — here indicating he gave the instruction concerning that letter. در کار افراسیاب is a prepositional phrase 'in/with regard to the affair/matter of Afrasiab', where در کار (in the affair) specifies the letter's purpose or destination.
segments.s311-g5: چو شد is a temporal clause 'when it became'. خامه (the pen/quill) is the subject. The comparative construction uses از to set the standard of comparison: 'خامه از مشک ... قیر‌تر' = 'the pen became more (like) musk than (like) tar.' وز here is a poetic/archaic connective (a fused or clipped form) linking the two comparison terms; it does not match the modern dictionary senses listed and functions like 'than/than of' in this line.
segments.s311-g6: نخست 'first' is an adverb modifying the following clause. آفرین کرد بر دادگر is an idiomatic predicate: آفرین (an exclamation/noun 'praise, acclamation') with کردن = 'to give praise', and بر دادگر = 'to/upon the just one' (بر marks the target). Together: 'he first praised the just one.' کرد is the simple past 'did/made'.
segments.s311-g7: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/for'), giving attributes of the previously praised subject. دارنده is an agent noun 'holder/possessor', followed by و and another agent phrase بر سر آرنده — a poetic participial phrase from بر سر آوردن 'to place upon the head' (i.e. 'to crown' or 'to set on the head'), meaning 'the one who crowns/sets on the head'. اوست is the copula 'he is' closing the clause.
segments.s311-g8: This clause states another predicate about the same subject with fronting of the object: زمین و زمان را (earth and time + را as definite/object marker) followed by نگارنده اوست 'he is the writer/inscriber'. The object-marker را with the fronted noun phrase emphasizes the domain over which he is 'the writer' (i.e. he authors/records earth and time). اوست is again the copula 'he is'.
segments.s311-g73: Structure: subject + verb + prepositional phrase. تبیره is the subject (here a group/retinue or name — the text is ambiguous). بر آمد (usually written برآمد) is a past-tense verbal phrase 'came forth/appeared' with بر as a prefixed element; ز is the poetic form of از 'from' and introduces the place phrase ز درگاه شاه 'from the king's court/presence' where درگاه (followed by شاه) denotes the royal threshold/court.
segments.s311-g74: برفتند is a 3rd-person plural past 'they went/departed.' گردان here names the group ('troop/retinue') that is the subject. ب + دان together form بِدان = 'to that' (ب = to/into attached to a demonstrative), and بارگاه is the destination 'palace/royal hall.' Word order is somewhat inverted (verb before subject) as common in poetry.
segments.s311-g75: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign.' پس functions as a temporal/discourse adverb 'then' or 'afterwards.' گیو را is the definite object (را marks the object). پیش خواند is a compound verbal idea 'called forward / summoned before [him]' with پیش as a directional/adverbial element and خواند the past-tense verb 'called/summoned.'
segments.s311-g76: This is a locative phrase + verb of causation: بر 'on/upon' introduces the location; آن نامور تخت شاهی is 'that famed royal throne' (نامور modifies تخت, شاهی qualifies it as royal), and نشاند is the archaic transitive past meaning 'he seated (him)' — i.e. he caused Giv to sit on the throne. The object (Giv) is carried over from the previous clause and thus is not repeated here.
segments.s311-g77: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / that,' and خواسته is 'the request/petition.' پیش برد is the verbal idea 'to bring forward / to present' where پیش is directional and برد is the past of بردن 'to carry/bring' (here used in the sense 'present'). The clause expresses an order: 'He ordered that the request be presented.'
segments.s311-g78: This short noun group is descriptive/ identificatory: همان 'that/same' + نامور 'renowned' + سرفرازان 'the proud/victorious ones.' گرد here is a terse element often used with groups in epic diction; it functions to indicate the band/assembly (the exact nuance is poetic). The phrase names or points to the group relevant to the command.
segments.s311-g79: A noun phrase: همان 'that same' modifies بیگنه‌رو/بیگنه روی 'strange- or unfamiliar-faced' (بیگنه = strange/unfamiliar; روی = face), and پوشیدگان is a participial noun 'the veiled/covered ones.' Together they form a descriptive group: 'those same strange-faced, veiled people.'
segments.s311-g80: پس is a discourse/temporal connector 'then' or 'afterwards.' پرده اندر is a locative phrase 'behind the curtain' (اندر = in/within). ستم دیدگان is a compound meaning 'those who have suffered oppression' (literally 'oppression-seen') — a fixed poetic expression for 'the oppressed.' Grammatically, ستم (oppression) modifies دیدگان, which is a participial/nominal form denoting persons affected.
segments.s311-g25: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بر پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before God' (بر + پیش together mark orientation toward someone). گشایی is a 2nd‑person singular classical/poetic present/subjunctive form of گشودن 'to open' and governs the following object دو لب ('both lips' = the mouth). Word order is subordinating clause: چو + phrase + verb + object.
segments.s311-g26: نیایش کن is an imperative construction: نیایش (prayer/supplication) + کن (imperative 'do') = 'pray'. از بهر من is a prepositional phrase 'for my sake' (از ... بهر = 'for / on behalf of'), and روز و شب is a fixed adverbial 'day and night' meaning continually. The verb کن scope-captures the entire following prepositional phrase.
segments.s311-g27: کشیدیم is the 1st‑person plural past tense 'we drew/marched' used in epic narration. لشکر is the direct object 'army'. به introduces the destination 'to/toward', and ماچین و چین are place names; poetically the place names follow the preposition. The clause is a simple past narrative statement.
segments.s311-g28: وز آن is an archaic/conjunctive sequence meaning 'and from that / thereupon' (وز here is an older connective rather than the common noun 'gust'); روی رانم = 'I set my face / I rode / I drove' where روی 'face/direction' marks the direction and رانم is a 1st‑person form of راندن 'to drive/ride' (classical narrative use). به مکران زمین = 'toward the land of Makran' (به + place). Overall this gives the narrative motion from the previous clause toward Makran.
segments.s311-g29: وز آن پس is a temporal sequence 'and after that' (پس = 'after/then'). بر introduces the path 'over/above' (بر آب = 'over the water' / 'across the water'). زره is an object in juxtaposition indicating 'with armor' and بگذرم is a 1st‑person subjunctive/potential of گذشتن 'to pass' (here meaning 'I may/can cross' or 'I crossed', classical narrative uses subjunctive-like forms for potential/future action). The sentence expresses willingness/possibility of crossing the water armored.
segments.s311-g30: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. پای یزدان literally 'the foot of God' is an idiomatic way to speak of divine support or power; بود is the past form of 'to be' (here functioning in the protasis as 'were / was'), and یاورم is 'my helper' (یاور + possessive suffix -م). Together: 'if God's foot were my helper' i.e. 'if God aided me'.
segments.s311-g31: ز پیش شهنشاه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from before the shah/emperor' (ز = poetic 'from', پیش = 'in the presence of'). برگشت is the past verb 'returned', placed before the subject in this verb‑initial classical style, and گیو is the subject (the proper name). The clause is a past‑tense narrative statement.
segments.s311-g32: ابا here functions as an archaic/prepositional element meaning 'with' or 'accompanied by' (a usage not reflected in the provided noun‑senses list). لشکری 'an army/troops' is followed by the adjective گشن 'hungry' (in poetic prose adjectives often follow the noun). و links to مردان نیو 'and new men' (نیو = 'new' in this context). The string describes Giv's retinue: a hungry army and fresh/young men.
segments.s311-g89: پس is an adverb meaning 'then/afterwards' and opens the clause. پرده شاهشان is a genitive phrase (parde-ye shāhshān) 'the curtain of their king' with the possessor attached to the noun. جای کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase: جای کردن here means 'to place/put in its place' and کرد is the simple past auxiliary 'did/put'. The verb follows its object phrase, as common in Persian.
segments.s311-g90: همانگه is an adverb 'at that moment/instantly'. پرستنده is the direct object 'the worshipper/devotee'. بر پای کردن is a light causative construction: بر (on/upon) + پای (foot) + کردن (to make) yields 'to set/make (someone) on their feet' = 'to make (someone) stand'. The verb کرد is the past tense 'made'. Note the object (پرستنده) appears before the verb.
segments.s311-g91: اسیران is a plural noun 'the captives' and is coordinated with the following noun phrase by و ('and'). آن کس که بود از نوا is a relative clause: آن کس (that person) + که (who) + بود (was, past copula) + از نوا ('of/from song' meaning 'skilled in song' or 'a singer'). Word order places the relative clause after the head noun as usual.
segments.s311-g92: بیاراست is a past tense verb 'he adorned/dressed'. مر is an enclitic dative/benefactive 'to me/for me' placed directly after the verb. هر یکی را is the definite object 'each one' marked by را. جدا is an adverb 'separately', modifying the verb. Note the verb appears before its indirect object (مر) and the definite direct object is marked by را.
segments.s311-g93: The pattern یکی را ... یکی را ... presents two objects in parallel. Each یکی را marks 'one [person]' as the definite object. نگهبان here functions as a predicate nominal without a copula—'(they made) one (be) a guard'—while به بند uses به + بند ('into fetters/into bondage') to indicate someone being put in chains. Persian often omits an explicit verb here, understood from the following فعل.
segments.s311-g94: ببردند is the simple past plural 'they took/led' (here referring to the attendants). از پیش + [person] is an idiomatic locative phrase meaning 'into the presence of' or 'before' someone; thus از پیش شاه بلند = 'into the presence of the lofty/tall king'. بلند is an epithet 'lofty/lofty-standing'. The subject is implicit in the verb's plural ending.
segments.s311-g95: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'from that after/then on'. همه خواسته هرچه بود is a nominalized clause: همه خواسته 'all (people)'s wishes' or 'everyone's request(s)', followed by هرچه بود 'whatever it was'. بود is the past copula 'was' anchoring the relative content in the past.
segments.s311-g96: ز دینار وز گوهر lists the kinds of request: 'of dinars and of gems' (ز is a poetic variant of از 'of/from'). وز appears here as a fused conjunctional element (archaic 'and of'), joining the two items. نابسود is a predicate meaning 'not fruitless/was not to no avail' i.e. 'was not refused/was granted' in context. The whole phrase qualifies the previous 'whatever was asked' as being supplied in money or jewels.
segments.s311-g65: می‌آورد is a 3rd-person-singular imperfective/past-habitual form ('he would bring' / 'he brought in') used narratively; و simply joins the two actions; رامشگران is the plural definite noun 'peacemakers/comforters' followed by the object marker را; بخواند is a 3rd-person-singular past/subjunctive form here meaning 'he summoned' (classical Persian uses خواندن = 'to call/summon' in addition to 'read'). The clause order is verb — object (را-marked) — verb (repeated in the next clause) common in epic Persian.
segments.s311-g66: وز here functions as a contracted poetic sequence (equivalent to 'و از' = 'and from'), introducing a source; ایران names the place; نبرده is a negative past participle/verb form ('not taken' / 'had not been taken'); سران را is the object 'the chiefs/leaders' with the object marker را; بخواند repeats the verb 'he summoned.' The phrase orders the source ('from Iran') before the participle and object in a typically inverted classical style.
segments.s311-g67: ز هر گونه‌ای is a prepositional phrase 'of every kind / in every manner' (ز = 'of/from' in poetic register); گفت is the 3rd-person-singular past of گفتن ('he spoke' / 'he said'); و links the second verb; پاسخ is the noun 'answer/reply' and شنید is the past tense 'he heard.' Both verbs are simple past, parallel actions (he spoke and he heard answers).
segments.s311-g68: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the following clause; تا here means 'until'; شب تیره = 'the dark night' (adjective after noun is classical but here word order is noun + adjective); اندر is a poetic preposition 'in/within'; چمید is an archaic past form meaning 'closed in / settled / drew in' (an older verb form used poetically). The clause states that the action continued up to the coming in of the dark night.
segments.s311-g69: برفتند is the 3rd-person-plural past 'they went/they departed'; با introduces accompanying means 'with' (شمع یاران = 'candles [and] companions' where شمع is 'candle' and یاران 'companions'); ز پیش literally 'from before' / 'from in front of' (ز = 'from', پیش here means 'in the presence of' or 'in front of') indicating the point they left. Word order is verb — adjunct ('with ...') — source ('from ...').
segments.s311-g70: دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + suffix ش = 'his'); shاد و خرم are two adjectives linked by و meaning 'glad and joyful'; به ایوان خویش is a prepositional phrase 'to/at his own iwan/portico' (به = 'to/toward', ایوان = 'portico', خویش = 'his own' with خوی + ش split in the word list). Note the copula 'was' is omitted (common in Persian), so the NP functions predicatively.
segments.s311-g71: چو is a classical subordinating conjunction 'when'; برزد is an archaic/perfomative past-form verb meaning here roughly 'fell/rose/struck' in the sense 'the sun moved/was revealed'; خور is the noun 'sun'; از چرخ = 'from the sky' (از = 'from', چرخ = 'sky/heaven'); رخشان modifies چرخ as 'bright/illustrious'; سنان is the noun 'spear' (here likely a poetic epithet or image). The clause is a temporal image ('when the sun of the bright sky...').
segments.s311-g72: بپیچید is a past-tense verb 'he turned/twisted' (بـ- prefix + پیچیدن); شب گرد is a compound/descriptor 'the night having come/around' (گرد = 'around' / 'about'); کرده is the past participle 'having made/done' used here in a periphrastic construction modifying عنان; عنان is 'reins/bridle.' Together the clause describes the night closing in and the rider turning the reins — a compact poetic sequence using participial and past forms.
segments.s311-g97: به is the dative preposition 'to/for' marking the indirect beneficiary; ارزانیان is a plural noun 'the poor/the cheap ones' receiving the gift; داد is a simple past verb 'he gave'. تا begins a purpose clause ('so that') and introduces the following verb phrase; آفرین here is a noun 'acclamation/praise' that will be the content of the purpose clause.
segments.s311-g98: بخوانند is a 3rd-person plural verbal form with the b- prefix and -ند ending used here as a subjunctive/jussive expressing purpose after تا ('so that they may/should sing/acclaim'); بر is a preposition 'to/toward' marking the target of the acclamation; شاه ایران‌زمین is the definite noun phrase 'the Shah of the land of Iran' receiving the praise.
segments.s311-g99: دگر here means 'other/a different'; بردگان is the plural 'slaves/servants' (the thing handed over); مهتران is the plural form meaning 'elders/chiefs' (in poetry the modifier can follow), and را is the definite object marker attaching to the noun phrase; سپرد is the past transitive 'he entrusted/handed over'. The whole phrase describes a past act of entrusting.
segments.s311-g100: به ایوان is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the iwan' (audience-hall); ببرد is a b- prefixed verb form here narratively meaning 'he brought/took' (3rd-person past/optative shape used in epic styles); از بزرگان و خرد supplies the source/constituents 'from the great (men) and the wise'—بزرگان 'great ones' and خرد used as a collective 'the wise' or 'wisdom-people'.
segments.s311-g101: بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they adorned/arranged'; از در جهن is a locative phrase 'at/from the gate of Jahan' where از + در (here 'gate') names the place and جهن is a proper name (genitive relation); جای is the noun 'place' that is being arranged by the verb.
segments.s311-g102: خورش is a noun 'a cooked dish/food' specifying what they provided; با is the preposition 'with' linking the supplied items; پرستنده and رهنمای are nouns 'attendants/devotees' and 'guides' coordinated by و. The sequence lists accompaniment/items placed at the arranged spot.
segments.s311-g103: به دژ is a locative prepositional phrase 'at the fortress'; بر یکی جای is a poetic order meaning 'on one (side) a place' where بر expresses position and یکی functions as 'a certain/one'; تاریک is an adjective 'dark' modifying جای; بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s311-g104: ز دل دور is a short locational phrase 'far from the heart/center' (ز used poetically for 'from', دل here 'heart/center'); با دخمه نزدیک بود continues with a contrast 'but it was near a دخمه' (دخمه = vault/sepulchre/dungeon); نزدیک بود is the past-state 'was near.'
segments.s311-g121: Literal word order is somewhat poetic: درم (a coin-name) appears before the verb داد (past 'gave'); the conjunction و links items (here two kinds of money). داد is the past tense verb (3rd-person) whose action ('gave') applies to both monetary items. درویش را is a definite/accusative object phrase marked by را ('to the dervish').
segments.s311-g122: پراگنده functions as a predicative/adverbial adjective meaning 'scattered' or 'widely distributed' and is attached to the preceding verb by ellipsis (داد is understood to govern it). و connects this idea to the next object phrase. مردم خویش را is the object phrase 'his own people' where خوی + ش (=خویش) gives 'own/kin' and را again marks it as definite/accusative; the verb is omitted here but understood from داد.
segments.s311-g123: به دو هفته expresses a temporal span ('for two weeks'); هفته = 'week'. در پیش is an idiomatic/poetic locution meaning 'in the presence of' or 'before' and combines with درگاه شاه ('the royal court / the king's presence') to mean 'at the king's court'. Word order places the temporal phrase before the locative phrase.
segments.s311-g124: از introduces a cause/source ('from' or 'because of') and governs the noun phrase انبوه بخشش ('the crowd/abundance of giving'); ندیدند is the past negative ('they did not see' / 'they could not find') and راه is the noun 'way/path' functioning as the object of ندیدند. The verb comes after its object phrase in typical Persian order here.
segments.s311-g125: سیم هفته (an older/poetic form meaning 'the third week') gives the temporal setting. بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; جایگاه مهی is a locative noun phrase 'the moon‑like / resplendent seat/throne' (مهی is a poetic epithet). The whole phrase means 'in the third week upon the splendid throne.'
segments.s311-g126: نشست here is the past intransitive verb 'sat' (3rd person). اندر آرام is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in peace / quietly'; با introduces a comitative phrase 'with' and فرهی ('the nobles' / 'the noble ones') is the companion. The subject is implicit; the verb governs the following phrases.
segments.s311-g127: ز بس is a poetic causative/quantifying phrase ('from/for so much'); it introduces the causes that follow. The nouns form possessive/compound relations without an explicit ezafe: ناله نای = 'the moan of the ney (reed‑flute)' and بانگ سرود = 'the sound/cry of the song.' The omission of ezafe is typical in classical/poetic Persian compounds.
segments.s311-g128: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually / was constantly'; داد is the past verb 'gave.' گل جام می‌را is a compounded nominal phrase acting as the subject (literally 'the flower/darling of the cup — Mi‑rā'), with genitive relationship expressed poetically; درود is the direct object 'greeting' or 'salutation.' The verb therefore means 'Mi‑rā (the cup's flower) continually gave greeting.'
